coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dot fluorescent probe for labeling and imaging of stellate cell on liver frozen section below freezing point
coredata.dc:creator: He, Yan-Fei
Cheng, Kai
Zhong, Zi-Tao
Hou, Xiao-Lin
An, Chang-Zhi
Zhang, Jing
Chen, Wei
Liu, Bo
Yuan, Jing
Zhao, Yuan-Di
coredata.dc:description: The targeted labeling imaging of stellate cells on liver frozen section by immunofluorescence is a very promising visualization technique to study the distribution of stellate cells in the liver. In this study, water soluble carbon quantum dots that can emit blue, green and yellow fluorescence are synthesized by the hydrothermal method, and their sizes are 3.2, 3.7, and 4.3 nm, respectively. The three carbon quantum dots have good fluorescence stability, and the quantum yields are 36.1%, 26.3% and 21%, respectively. When the mass fraction of KCl in the blue carbon quantum dot dispersion system is 13%, it still maintains the liquid state at −30 °C. The final fluorescent probe is obtained after the carbon quantum dots are coupled with the secondary antibody, spectral characterizations confirm that the conjugate probe still maintains protein immunoactivity and has good stability. Cell experiments prove that the probe has good biocompatibility, the rabbit anti-mouse Desmin antibody is used as the primary antibody, the results of cellular immunofluorescence imaging and flow cytometry show that the probe can specifically label hepatic stellate cell at −20 °C. The results of liver frozen section experiments show that hepatic stellate cell can be specifically targeted and labeled by the fluorescent probe. This labeling technology provides an important technical means for elucidating the structure and function of the liver at the cellular level, exploring the liver pathological change, and designing and developing drug.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Freezing point
Hepatic stellate cell
Bioimaging
Liver slice

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dot-conjugated goat anti-rabbit IgG probe (CDs-Ab)
Selection of cell low temperature environment
Low-temperature fluorescence imaging of hepatic stellate cells
In vitro toxicity analysis of cells
Fluorescence imaging of hepatic stellate cells on frozen section
Low freezing point CDs dispersion system
Low-temperature fluorescence imaging of hepatic stellate cells
Fluorescence imaging of hepatic stellate cells frozen sections

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFA0700402), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 81971658). We also thank the Analytical and Testing Center (HUST), the Research Core Facilities for Life Science (HUST) and the Center for Nanoscale Characterization & Devices (CNCD) at WNLO of HUST for the help of measurement.

This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2017YFA0700402 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 81971658 ). We also thank the Analytical and Testing Center (HUST), the Research Core Facilities for Life Science (HUST) and the Center for Nanoscale Characterization & Devices (CNCD) at WNLO of HUST for the help of measurement.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of B-CDs (A), G-CDs (B) and Y-CDs (C), inset: hydrated particle size distribution.
X-ray photoelectron spectra (A), X-ray diffraction patterns (B), Raman spectra (C) and fluorescence decay curves (D) of B-CDs (a), G-CDs (b) and Y-CDs (c).
Absorption (A) and fluorescence (B) spectra of B-CDs (a), G-CDs (b) and Y-CDs (c), inset: the object fluorescence photo under UV light.
Fluorescence spectra of B-CDs (A), G-CDs (B) and Y-CDs (C) at different excitation wavelengths.
The fluorescence spectra at different pH (A: B-CDs; B: G-CDs; C: Y-CDs) and different concentrations (D: B-CDs; E: G-CDs; F: Y-CDs); quantum yield (G) and photobleaching resistance (H) of CDs.

Physical photo and fluorescence imaging of B-CDs dispersion system containing different concentrations of KCl (A and E), MgCl2 (B and F), NaCl (C and G) and CaCl2 (D and H) at −30 °C; fluorescence spectra of B-CDs dispersion system containing 13% KCl (a), 14% MgCl2 (b), 13% NaCl (c) and 12% CaCl2 (d) at 37 °C (I) and −30 °C (J), and salt free (e) at 37 °C.

Temperature-dependent spectra of B-CDs(A), G-CDs(B) and Y-CDs(C), curve a: the absorption spectrum at −30 °C, curves b–h: fluorescence emission spectra at 0, -5, -10, −15, −20, −25 and −30 °C, respectively.
Temperature dependence of normalized fluorescence intensity (A), quantum yield (B), peak wavelength (C) and half-peak width (B) of B-CDs (a), G-CDs (b) and Y-CDs (c).
Fluorescence intensities of B-CDs (A), G-CDs (B) and Y-CDs (C) under heating (0 °C)-cooling (−30 °C) cycle.
Absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs-Ab (A); agarose gel electrophoresis (B), zeta potential (C) and capillary electrophoresis (D) of B-CDs before and after conjugation with Ab.
Zeta potential and hydrated particle size (A), polydispersity index PDI (B), photobleaching resistance (C) and circular dichroism (D) of CDs-Ab.

Hepatic stellate cells (A) and their cell viability after incubation with different concentrations of CDs-Ab probe at −20 °C (B), n = 7; the bright field and fluorescence images of hepatic stellate cells treated with or without anti-Desmin primary antibody after labeling with CDs-Ab probe at −20 °C (C).
The bright field and fluorescence images of hepatic cells treated with or without anti-Desmin primary antibody after labeling with CDs-Ab probe at −20 °C.

Flow cytometric analysis of blank hepatic stellate cells (A), hepatic stellate cells incubated with anti-Desmin primary antibody and then FITC-Ab (B) or CDs-Ab (C) at - 20 °C, and hepatic stellate cells incubated with CDs-Ab without primary antibody (D).
Bright field, fluorescence imaging and fluorescence spectrum of hepatic stellate cells labeled with CDs-Ab and FITC-Ab on liver sections at −20 °C and 37 °C.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dot fluorescent probe for labeling and imaging of stellate cell on liver frozen section below freezing point

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The targeted labeling imaging of stellate cells on liver frozen section by immunofluorescence is a very promising visualization technique to study the distribution of stellate cells in the liver. In this study, water soluble carbon quantum dots that can emit blue, green and yellow fluorescence are synthesized by the hydrothermal method, and their sizes are 3.2, 3.7, and 4.3 nm, respectively. The three carbon quantum dots have good fluorescence stability, and the quantum yields are 36.1%, 26.3% and 21%, respectively. When the mass fraction of KCl in the blue carbon quantum dot dispersion system is 13%, it still maintains the liquid state at −30 °C. The final fluorescent probe is obtained after the carbon quantum dots are coupled with the secondary antibody, spectral characterizations confirm that the conjugate probe still maintains protein immunoactivity and has good stability. Cell experiments prove that the probe has good biocompatibility, the rabbit anti-mouse Desmin antibody is used as the primary antibody, the results of cellular immunofluorescence imaging and flow cytometry show that the probe can specifically label hepatic stellate cell at −20 °C. The results of liver frozen section experiments show that hepatic stellate cell can be specifically targeted and labeled by the fluorescent probe. This labeling technology provides an important technical means for elucidating the structure and function of the liver at the cellular level, exploring the liver pathological change, and designing and developing drug.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time as the data also forms part of an ongoing study.

ce:section.ce:para: Hepatic stellate cell, also known as fat storage cell, sinusoidal pericyte, Ito cell, etc., is located between hepatic sinusoidal endothelial cells and liver parenchyma cells, and has many functions such as storing vitamin A, regulating intrahepatic blood flow, and secreting cytokines [1], and involves in regulating the pathophysiological processes of various liver diseases. When the liver is damaged by inflammation or mechanical stimulation, stellate cell will synthesize numerous extracellular matrix, leading to the formation of liver fibrosis, which eventually develops into liver cirrhosis [2] and liver failure [3]. Therefore, studying the distribution of stellate cells in the liver can help us deeply understand the structure and function of the liver, clarify the pathogenesis of the disease and provide help for the treatment.

The localization of stellate cells in liver tissue can be realized based on the specific recognition between antibody and antigen. Common cell labeling techniques include immunohistochemistry [4], immunofluorescence [5], western blotting [6] and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [7], etc, among them, immunofluorescence has attracted much attention due to its high detection sensitivity, strong specificity, and simple operation. Common tissue sectioning methods include paraffin section, ultra-thin section, vibrating section, frozen section, etc, [8–10]. Among them, the frozen section has received high attention due to its advantages such as high antigenic immune activity, strong specificity, and simple steps [11]. Therefore, the targeted labeling imaging of stellate cells on liver frozen section by immunofluorescence is a very promising visualization technique to study the distribution of stellate cells in the liver.

Organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots are often used to prepare fluorescent probes for cell fluorescent imaging. However, the former suffer from poor fluorescence stability, while the latter usually use heavy metals as raw materials resulting in poor biocompatibility, which limits their application in bioimaging. Carbon quantum dots (CDs), as new type of zero-dimensional carbon material, have a size between 1 and 10 nm. Due to their high fluorescence stability, good biocompatibility, simple preparation method, cheap and readily available raw materials (most carbon sources are natural green carbon-based materials) [12–14], they have been widely used in bioimaging, biosensing, optoelectronic devices and other fields [15–17]. The synthesis methods of CDs include electrochemical oxidation method, arc discharge method, hydrothermal method, laser ablation method, microwave irradiation method, etc. [18–20]. Among them, the hydrothermal method is widely used by researchers owing to its friendly environment, simple operation and mild reaction conditions. At present, it was found that fluorescent CDs with different colors can be synthesized by changing the solvent ratio, temperature, time and other factors, for example, Ren et al. [21] synthesized blue, green and red CDs by changing the reaction temperature in ethanol solvent with 2-aminothiophenol and citric acid as raw materials; Song et al. [22] used citric acid and urea as carbon and nitrogen sources respectively, obtained blue, green, yellow, orange and red CDs by adjusting the ratio of raw materials, temperature and time. Because most multicolor carbon quantum dots need to be synthesized in organic solvents, such as ethanol [23], DMF [24], acetone [25], etc., it is difficult to apply in biological environment. And the fluorescence of the water-soluble carbon quantum prepared by the same material does not change with the reaction temperature, time, solvent ratio and other factors. Therefore, we achieved multicolor emission of carbon quantum by adopting different raw materials and using water as the solvent according to Ref. [26].

Therefore, based on their excellent fluorescence characteristics, carbon quantum dots can be used for fluorescent probes in biological imaging, biosensing and other fields. For example, Pang et al. synthesized non-doped and non-modified green carbon dots with high quantum yield and applied to HeLa cell imaging [27]. Huang et al. synthesized carbon spots by hydrothermal method using by-products of biorerefining as precursors, and applied them to imaging of mouse myeloma cells and in vivo SMMC-7721 tumor cells [28]. Cao et al. synthesized a simple, green and low-cost multifunctional CDs with yeast powder precursor and applied it to dopamine detection and MCF-7 cell imaging [29]. Bai et al. prepared a new type of carbon quantum dots with mitochondria-targeting function via a simple one-step hydrothermal route, the as-prepared carbon quantum dots acting as a functional optical probe through a self-targeting mechanism were successfully applied for in situ visualization of endogenous peroxynitrite generated in the mitochondria of live cells [30].

In this paper, blue, green and yellow CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method with different carbon sources, and immunofluorescent probe was prepared by coupling with antibody. The probe had excellent fluorescence performance and showed specific targeted labeling ability below the freezing point, it could carry out sensitive immunofluorescence labeling imaging to study the distribution of stellate cells in liver section, showing that the probe presented great application prospect in the fields of cell labeling and imaging, at the same time, it also provided an important technical means for exploring liver structure and function.

Glutathione (GSH, 98%), o-phenylenediamine (99%), p-phenylenediamine (98%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich; citric acid, potassium chloride, calcium chloride, magnesium chloride and sodium chloride were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Goat anti-rabbit IgG, FITC goat anti-rabbit IgG, and rabbit anti-mouse Desmin (anti-Desmin) were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., Ltd.; UFC505096 ultrafiltration centrifuge tube was purchased from Millipore (USA), other reagents were analytically pure products, and the water used in the experiment was ultrapure water.

The instruments used in the experiment include HT7700 biological transmission electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan), Nano-ZS90 nm particle sizer (Malvern, United Kingdom), Elx-808 microplate reader (Biotek, United States), QE6500 fluorescence spectrometer (Ocean Optics, United States), UV-2550 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), IX71 inverted fluorescence microscope (Olympus, Japan), LS-55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA), AXIS-ULTRA DLD-600W X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos, Japan), X'Pert PRO X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical B.V., Netherlands), LabRAM HR800 laser confocal Raman spectrometer (Horiba JobinYvon, France), QuantaMaster 8000 steady state transient modular fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba, Canada), MS-100 constant temperature mixer (Hangzhou Aosheng Instrument Co., Ltd., China), DLSB5-40 °C cryogenic coolant circulation pump (Henan Jinbo Instrument Manufacturing Co., Ltd., China), 5702 horizontal centrifuge (Eppendorf, Germany), Cyto FLEX flow cytometer (Beckman Coulter, USA).

0.1 g citric acid and 0.1 g o-phenylenediamine, 0.1 g o-phenylenediamine, 10 mg glutathione, and 20 mg p-phenylenediamine were dissolved in 15 mL ultra-pure water ultrasonically, respectively. They were placed in a 25 mL polytetrafluoroethylene high-pressure reaction kettle and reacted at 200 °C in a muffle furnace for 4 h, after cooling naturally to room temperature, the reactants were collected and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, the supernatant solution was filtered through 0.22 μm filter membrane, and then purified for 48 h through dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 500 D, the ultrapure water was replaced every 4–6 h. Finally, the solid lyophilized powders were obtained by freeze-drying and dissolved in a certain amount of PBS to obtain blue fluorescent carbon quantum dots (B-CDs), green fluorescent carbon quantum dots (G-CDs), and yellow fluorescent carbon quantum dots (Y-CDs).

B-CDs, G-CDs, and Y-CDs were excited at different wavelengths (300, 320, 340, 360, 380, 400, 420 and 440 nm), different pH (4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 and 10), and different concentrations (10, 20, 50, 100, 150, 200 and 300 μg/mL), respectively, the fluorescence spectra were measured, and the changing trend of the emission peak position was investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dot-conjugated goat anti-rabbit IgG probe (CDs-Ab)
ce:section.ce:para: B-CDs, EDC and sulfo-NHS were dissolved in MES buffer with pH 5.6 at a ratio of 1:200:400, after stirring at room temperature for 15 min, the pH of the reaction system was adjusted to 7.2–7.4 with 1 M NaOH. A certain amount of goat anti-rabbit IgG (Ab) was added and reacted at room temperature in the dark for 2 h. After the reaction, the unreacted substance was removed with a 50 K ultrafiltration tube and dissolved in PBS, which was stored at 4 °C in the dark.

The excellent photobleaching resistance of the probe could realize long-term bioimaging, Therefore, CDs-Ab and rhodamine 6G were placed at 30 cm under a benchtop ZF-6 UV lamp (25W, Shanghai Jiapeng Technology Co., Ltd.) and continuously irradiated under 365 nm excitation light for 1 h. The fluorescence spectra were measured at 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 min, respectively, and three parallels were set for each group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of cell low temperature environment
ce:section.ce:para: Various chlorides (KCl, NaCl, MgCl2 and CaCl2)/B-CDs dispersion systems with different mass fractions (5%, 10%, 11%, 12%, 13%, 14%, 15% and 20%) were prepared and placed in a −30 °C low-temperature cooling tank and kept at constant temperature for 12 h overnight, the solution state was observed the next day, and the white light and fluorescence imaging of the B-CDs quantum dot dispersion system were taken respectively, the fluorescence spectrum at 365 nm was recorded.

Balb/c female mouse aged 5 weeks was selected, the liver blood was removed by portal vein perfusion after alcohol disinfection and intraperitoneal injection anesthesia, after in vivo collagenase digestion, continued in vitro digestion in 37 °C water bath for 10 min, single cell suspension was obtained by 70 μm cell sieve filtration, the hepatocyte parenchyma cells with high density were removed by differential centrifugation, and then the hepatic stellate cells were obtained by density gradient horizontal centrifugation. The cells were added to a 96-well plate covered with cell climbing sheets and incubated in 37 °C, 5% CO2 incubator for 4 h, the medium was changed after the hepatic stellate cells adhered, and the culture was continued overnight, the following experiments were carried out the next day. All animal experiments were approved by the Animal Experiment Ethics Committee of Huazhong University of Science and Technology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Low-temperature fluorescence imaging of hepatic stellate cells
ce:section.ce:para: After the extracted hepatic stellate cells were cultured overnight, they were labeled by indirect immunofluorescence method at low temperature. After glutaraldehyde fixation, TX-100 permeability, and BSA blocking at 37 °C, rabbit anti-mouse Desmin antibody (anti-Desmin) as the primary antibody, 100 μL DMEM solution containing anti-Desmin (containing a certain amount of antifreeze) was directly added and incubated overnight at −20 °C, and washed with PBS three times. Ab conjugated with CDs was used as the secondary antibody, then CDs-Ab conjugate was added and incubated for 2 h at −20 °C, washed with PBS for 3 times, and nuclei were stained with PI in the dark for 15 min, the imaging of hepatic stellate cells was carried out quickly at room temperature. In the control experiment, the same concentration of CDs-Ab conjugate was directly added without a primary antibody as the negative control, and the fluorescence imaging of hepatic stellate cells was observed under the inverted fluorescence microscope.

After hepatic stellate cells were cultured at 37 °C overnight, the medium was removed and washed with PBS 3 times, then anti-Desmin and FITC-Ab probe were added as the control group, and anti-Desmin and CDs-Ab probe were used as the positive group, CDs-Ab with the same concentration was added separately as the negative group, they were incubated at −20 °C for 12 h, and then digested into single cell suspension by adding 0.25% trypsin after washed with PBS. The cells were collected by horizontal centrifugation and resuspended in PBS. 104 cells were quantitatively analyzed by flow cytometer, and the fluorescence intensity of hepatic stellate cells was detected to investigate the labeling efficiency of CDs-Ab probe on hepatic stellate cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro toxicity analysis of cells
ce:section.ce:para: The toxicity of the probe to cells was tested by MTT assay. The extracted primary hepatic stellate cells were seeded in a 96-well plate at a density of 1 × 105 and incubated at 37 °C overnight, washed 3 times with PBS, 100 μL serum-free medium containing the same concentration anti-Desmin was added as the primary antibody, and incubated at −20 °C for 12 h, then added 100 μL serum-free medium containing different concentration CDs-Ab (0, 10, 20, 50, 100, 150 and 200 μg/mL) after washed 3 times with PBS, after incubating at −20 °C for 2 h, the medium was aspirated and washed with PBS, 7 parallels for each group. 20 μL 5 mg/mL of thiazole blue (MTT) was added to each well, the medium was removed and cleaned with PBS after continuous culture for 4 h, and then 150 μL dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added, the cell plate was placed on the shaking table and vibrated slowly for 10 min, the absorption value at 490 nm was measured by a microplate reader, and calculated the cell survival rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging of hepatic stellate cells on frozen section
ce:section.ce:para: The mouse liver tissue block was placed on a pre-cooled round sample holder. The liver tissue was covered with an OCT embedding medium (be careful not to have air bubbles to prevent curling during subsequent sectioning) and then placed in a cryostat at −20 °C. When the sample turned white and solidified, the slice thickness was set to 10 μm, and it was cut continuously at a constant speed to get the liver frozen sections at −20 °C, which could adhere to the slide by the temperature difference between the anti-detachment slide and liver frozen section.

Liver frozen sections were placed at room temperature for 15 min, and the sections to be stained were circled by a super pap pen, the circle range was as small as possible to save the consumption of probe, the embedding medium around the circles was removed by soaking in PBS for 10 min, fixed with acetone, permeabilized with 0.3% TX-100, and blocked with 3% BSA serum at 37 °C, liver frozen sections were labeled by the fluorescence probe at −20 °C and 37 °C, respectively, the sections were observed at room temperature by fluorescence imaging system built by the Wuhan Optoelectronics National Laboratory [31], and the fluorescence spectra of the frozen sections were collected by fluorescence spectrometer to further verify the labeling effect.

CDs with three different colors of fluorescence emission were synthesized by the hydrothermal method and characterized to clear their morphology. The results showed that all CDs were approximately spherical (Fig. 1 ), with uniform dispersion and no obvious agglomeration. The particle size distribution was narrow, and the hydrated particle sizes were about 3.2 nm (Figs. 1A), 3.7 nm (Figs. 1B), and 4.3 nm (Fig. 1C) respectively.

In order to further verify the structure of CDs, they were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and the results showed that B-CDs, G-CDs and Y-CDs had the same elemental composition of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s (Fig. 2 A), and their characteristic peak positions were located at 281, 395 and 529 eV respectively, indicating that the CDs were composed of sp2 carbon cores and surface amorphous elements, the similar chemical composition suggested that the differences in their optical properties of different sizes were caused by the quantum confinement effect.

According to the XRD spectra, the characteristic peaks of B-CDs, G-CDs and Y-CDs were located at about 22.1°, 23.3° and 24° respectively (Fig. 2B), corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of graphite carbon, indicating that the interlayer stacking structure of CDs was similar to that of graphite carbon (≈0.35 nm), indicating that the their cores were mainly composed of tight pack of sp2 hybridization highly conjugated regions, therefore, CDs presented high crystallinity graphene structure.

According to the Raman spectra, CDs had two characteristic peaks (Fig. 2C), which were located at 1583 and 1338 cm−1 respectively, corresponding to the typical G-band and D-band in CDs, representing the amorphous structure and graphite carbon structure in CDs. The graphitization degree and sp2/sp3 carbon atom ratio of CDs were investigated by the ratio of G-band to D-band intensity (I G/I D), the results showed that the I G/I D ratios of B-CDs, G-CDs and Y-CDs were 0.985, 1.115 and 1.236 respectively, indicating that the sp2 conjugated structure of three CDs gradually increased, and the graphitization degree also gradually increased, which was closely related to the fluorescence emission peaks position; the above results showed that the CDs had a highly crystalline structure, it was consistent with XRD.

The fluorescence lifetime of traditional organic fluorescent dye was only a few nanoseconds, which was often plagued by biological autofluorescence. Therefore, the time-resolved fluorescence spectrum of CDs was also detected. The results showed that the fluorescence lifetimes of B-CDs, G-CDs and Y-CDs were 12.2, 11.6 and 9.4 ns respectively (Fig. 2D), indicating that CDs could more effectively inhibit the interference of spontaneous fluorescence.

After synthesizing water soluble CDs with different colors of fluorescence emission by different raw materials, UV–Vis absorption spectra showed that the absorption peaks of B-CDs, G-CDs and Y-CDs were broad and continuous (Fig. 3 A), near 331, 369 and 415 nm respectively, they belonged to the typical polycyclic aromatic π system, which was attributed to the n-π* transition of CN and the transition of sp2 hybrid orbital.

The fluorescence emission peaks of three CDs were wide (Fig. 3B), located at 430, 500 and 563 nm respectively, the maximum half peak widths were 73, 88 and 91 nm, and the stokes shifts were 99, 131 and 148 nm respectively. Large stokes shift could avoid spectral overlap and improved the detection sensitivity. The red shift of fluorescence emission peak was consistent with the red shift of absorption peak due to the capture of excited state energy by different quantum dot surface states, which could regulate the fluorescence emission of CDs. Three kinds of CDs generated bright blue, green and yellow fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 3B, inset), which showed that they could be used as excellent fluorescent labeling material.

The fluorescence of some CDs had excitation wavelength dependence, the emission wavelength would red-shift with the red-shift of the excitation wavelength, and this phenomenon limited their application in multicolor fluorescence imaging. Therefore, we also explored the fluorescence spectra of B-CDs (Fig. 4 A), G-CDs (Fig. 4B) and Y-CDs (Fig. 4C) at different excitation wavelengths (300, 320, 340, 360, 380, 400, 420 and 440 nm), the results showed that the emission wavelengths of the three CDs did not change with the increase of the excitation wavelength, exhibiting excitation wavelength independence, which might be due to the sufficient passivation of CDs surface, and the defects greatly decreased, so they had stable emission state. At the same time, it could be also seen that the fluorescence intensity changed with the red-shift of the excitation wavelength, indicating that the fluorescence was an eigenstate luminescence mode. However, it was also found that the optimal excitation wavelengths of three CDs were not consistent with the strongest absorption peak wavelengths (Fig. 3A), at present, the reason was still unclear, but this phenomenon had also been observed by other researchers [32,33].

At the same time, the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs at different pH (4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 and 10) were also characterized. The results showed that the pH had little effect on the emission peak positions of B-CDs (Fig. 5 A), G-CDs (Fig. 5B) and Y-CDs (Fig. 5C), and the variation range was very small, showing the consistency of their optical properties; it was also found that the three CDs had the strongest fluorescence in the neutral environment, which might be the result of the protonation and deprotonation of the surface molecules in the acidic and basic environments [34–36].

Besides the excitation wavelength and solution pH could affect the fluorescence performance of CDs, concentration was also an important factor, therefore, the fluorescence spectra of three kinds of CDs at different concentrations (10, 20, 50, 100, 150, 200 and 300 μg/mL) were also characterized. The results showed that when the concentration of CDs increased from 10 to 50 μg/mL, the fluorescence intensity also rose (Fig. 5D ∼ F), however when the concentration continued to increase to 300 μg/mL, the fluorescence intensity decreased with the increase of the concentration, it was conjectured when the concentration was too high, the CDs were close to each other and the spacing became smaller, resulting in energy transfer and fluorescence quenching, that meant aggregation-induced quenching. At the same time, the quantum yields of B-CDs, G-CDs and Y-CDs were also measured, and the results showed that with the red-shift of the CDs’ emission wavelength, the quantum yields also gradually decreased (Fig. 5G), they were 36.1%, 26.3%, and 21% respectively, which might be related to the different surface passivation degrees of CDs.

The fluorescence stability of the three CDs was also discussed. When they and organic fluorescent dye Rhodamine 6G (Rh6G) were all continuously irradiated under 365 nm ultraviolet light, it showed that the fluorescence intensity of Rh6G decreased significantly after 20 min (Fig. 5H), while those of the three CDs remained basically stable, even if after 1 h, they still maintained good fluorescence stability with about 80% of the original intensity, showing excellent photobleaching resistance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Low freezing point CDs dispersion system
ce:section.ce:para: When the cell was directly exposed to the environment below the freezing temperature, water molecule of inside and outside the cell would form ice crystal, which could destroy the cell's physical structure. Because liver frozen section could keep the integrity of RNA information, especially avoid the inactivation of proteins and enzymes, in order to maintain the integrity of cell surface antigen activity, the liver frozen section was obtained at −20 °C. According to this point, we also chose to specifically label the cells at −20 °C. In addition, the fluorescence performance of carbon quantum dots at −20 °C was significantly improved compared with that at 37 °C, it was more conducive to low-temperature fluorescence imaging. So this study intends to label and image hepatic stellate cells at −20 °C, therefore, it is necessary to reduce the freezing point of the labeling solution by adding antifreeze to keep it in a liquid state at −20 °C, so as to achieve the purpose of maintaining the cell morphology.

So in order to ensure that the CDs dispersion system could always maintain liquid state during the cooling process, four different salts (KCl, NaCl, MgCl2, and CaCl2) were chosen as antifreeze, and B-CDs were taken as an example to investigate the state of dispersion system at −30 °C under different salt concentrations, the results showed when the concentrations (mass fractions) of KCl, MgCl2, NaCl and CaCl2 were 13%, 14%, 13% and 12%, respectively, the system did not solidify even at −30 °C (Fig. 6A ∼ D), the solution could still flow to the bottom of the inverted EP tube with liquid state; fluorescence imaging results showed that the system exhibited bright blue fluorescence of B-CDs whether it was the liquid system or solidified state (Fig. 6E ∼ H).

Meanwhile, the fluorescence spectra of CDs dispersions containing 13% KCl, 14% MgCl2, 13% NaCl and 12% CaCl2 were collected, respectively. The results showed that after adding salt to the dispersion system, fluorescence decay appeared at 37 °C (Fig. 6 I), and the highest fluorescence intensity was in the CDs dispersion system containing 13% KCl. However, the fluorescence intensity at −30 °C was higher than 37 °C (Fig. 6J), indicating that low temperature was benefit for the increase of fluorescence intensity; similarly, the dispersion system containing 13% KCl showed the strongest fluorescence, which was even higher than the fluorescence intensity of the salt-free system at 37 °C (Fig. 6I, e). Therefore, in the subsequent low-temperature cell imaging, the culture medium was all contained 13% KCl.

Carbon quantum dots with 13% KCl were placed in low-temperature cooling tank at −30 °C for 12 h, and the structure of the carbon quantum dots was tested by XRD. The results showed that after the freeze, the characteristic peaks of B-CDs changed slightly from about 22.1° (Fig. 2B, a) to about 21.7° (Fig. S1), corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of graphite carbon. The above results showed that the change of temperature had little effect on the structure of carbon quantum dots, which was still graphene structure with high crystallinity.

To explore the temperature dependence of CDs, the absorption and emission spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs and Y-CDs were analyzed at different temperatures below the freezing point. The results showed that the absorption spectrum of CDs was not sensitive to temperature (Fig. 7 A∼C, a), and the absorption peak was almost unchanged (only the absorption spectrum at −30 °C was given), the fluorescence peak intensity of CDs showed a temperature dependence (Fig. 7Ã C, b ∼ h). With the decrease in temperature, the fluorescence intensity of the three CDs increased to some extent, it was guessed the decrease in temperature promoted the radiative transition, resulting in enhanced fluorescence emission. The reason for the fluorescence properties improvement at low temperature can be explained according to the literature [37]:
F L =
k 1
k 2
I 0
[ F ]
k 2
+
k 3
FL: fluorescence intensity, F: fluorophore in the ground state, I0: excitation light intensity, k1, k2, k3: rate constants.

Among them, k 3 is a non-radiative factor, and the change in temperature will affect the non-radiative transition, which will lead to the change of k 3, therefore, its change definitely results in the change of fluorescence intensity. The decrease in temperature inhibits the non-radiative transition, making the distribution of electrons and holes on the surface of the quantum dots more stable, resulting in a decrease in k 3, then the fluorescence intensity of the quantum dots will inevitably increase when they are excited by the same external energy.

After normalizing the fluorescence intensities of B-CDs, G-CDs and Y-CDs at different temperatures, it could be found that the fluorescence intensities of the three CDs all exhibited a good temperature dependence. When the temperature decreased, the fluorescence intensity was enhanced in varying degree, and the intensities at −30 °C increased to 147%, 135% and 122% of the original at 0 °C, respectively (Fig. 8 A); at the same time, the quantum yield also increased with the decrease of temperature (Fig. 8B); and the fluorescence emission peak wavelength shifted to the short wavelength direction to a certain extent, appeared a blue-shift phenomenon (Fig. 8C); and the half-peak width was correspondingly narrowed (Fig. 8D), which might be caused by the enhanced exciton-phonon coupling and trap-assisted relaxation [34]. Biju et al. considered that temperature would change the surface traps of CDs, so that fluorescence intensity would also change with temperature [38]. Meanwhile, the temperature also changed the interaction between the internal diodes of CDs [39], which made the fluorescence emission peak shift, therefore, the fluorescence properties of CDs possessed temperature-dependent.

The fluorescence reversibility of CDs was also an important index as a biomarker. The fluorescence spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs and Y-CDs were measured at 0 °C respectively, and then the temperature was reduced to −30 °C, and their fluorescence emission spectra were recorded, and the cycle was repeated for 5 times, with 5 parallels in each group. It was found that the fluorescence intensities at 0 °C and −30 °C did not change significantly after heating and cooling cycle (Fig. 9 ), and the fluorescence intensity at −30 °C was higher than 0 °C, indicating that low temperature would not irreversibly damage the fluorescence properties of CDs, and this fluorescence reversibility provided a strong guarantee for low temperature labeling using CDs.

It could be seen from Fig. 5 G-H, the results showed that B-CDs had the best quantum yield and fluorescence stability among the three CDs. Therefore, in subsequent experiments, B-CDs were selected as a fluorescent labeled probe for a really application. After it was coupled with goat anti-rabbit IgG (Ab) by coupling reaction, the absorption and fluorescence spectra of the CDs-Ab conjugate were characterized. The results showed that CDs-Ab had a strong absorption peak at 331 nm (Fig. 10 A), and the wavelength was almost identical to the position of individual B-CDs; the fluorescence emission peak of CDs-Ab was located at about 435 nm, which was about 5 nm red shift compared with that of B-CDs, owing to the larger particle size after coupling with Ab; at the same time, it was also found that the half-peak width became wider due to the different amount of B-CDs conjugated with Ab, resulting in nonuniform particle size during the coupling process, so the half-peak width became wider.

In order to verify whether the Ab was coupled to B-CDs, B-CDs and CDs-Ab were analyzed by agarose gel electrophoresis, respectively. It could be seen that the movement speed of CDs-Ab was slower than that of B-CDs (Fig. 10B), because the molar mass of B-CDs became larger after coupling with Ab, leading to the enhanced movement resistance to CDs-Ab in agarose gel, so the band of CDs-Ab lagged behind bare B-CDs, indicating that Ab was indeed coupled to the B-CDs.

To further verify the coupling effectiveness of CDs-Ab, B-CDs and CDs-Ab conjugates were analyzed with zeta potential, respectively. Since the B-CDs surface was rich in carboxyl groups, the surface potential was −38.2 ± 2.13 mV (Fig. 10C), indicating that the dispersion system had good stability; after coupling with the amino groups on Ab, since the negative charge on the surface of CDs-Ab decreased, the zeta potential reduced to −30.2 ± 1.22 mV, indicating that the Ab was indeed successfully conjugated with B-CDs.

Capillary electrophoresis analysis showed that the migration time of CDs-Ab was shorter than that of B-CDs alone (Fig. 10D), which was related to the different total negative charges on their surfaces. After the carboxyl groups on the surface of B-CDs were coupled with the amino groups on the Ab, the negative charge on the surface of the CDs-Ab conjugate was reduced, under the promotion of positive electroosmotic flow, the migration speed of the CDs-Ab conjugate was faster and had shorter electrophoretic migration time, therefore, the CDs-Ab conjugate was first detected; and the above results further indicated that the Ab was indeed conjugated to the B-CDs.

The stability of the probe determines the timeliness of cell imaging. Therefore, the CDs-Ab fluorescent probe was kept for 30 d, part of the solution was taken out to detect its zeta potential, hydrated particle size, and polydispersity index at 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 and 30 d, respectively. The results showed the zeta potential of the CDs-Ab remained around −30 mV even after 30 d (Fig. 11 A), and the hydrated particle size did not change significantly, indicating good stability; the polydispersity index fluctuated slightly around 0.2 (Fig. 11B), indicating that it had good dispersion and no obvious agglomeration. In order to explore the photobleaching resistance of CDs-Ab fluorescent probe, it was continuously irradiated under UV light for 1 h, and the fluorescence was collected at 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 and 60 min, the results showed that the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-Ab remained basically stable (Fig. 11C), indicating that the CDs-Ab probe had excellent fluorescence stability.

After Ab was coupled with B-CDs, whether the protein structure was damaged was the key to determine the specific binding between the probe and cells, therefore, the protein secondary structure was analyzed by circular dichroism. The results showed that the curves of Ab and CDs-Ab were basically consistent (Fig. 11D), indicating that the structure of the Ab protein was complete after coupling, and still maintained the Ab protein biological activity, which could be used for subsequent targeted labeling imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Low-temperature fluorescence imaging of hepatic stellate cells
ce:section.ce:para: Since it was necessary to examine the specific labeling of cells at −20 °C, therefore, the survival rate of hepatic stellate cells at −20 °C was also investigated by MTT assay. In the absence of fluorescent probe, hepatic stellate cells were cultured at −20 °C and 37 °C, respectively. The results showed that the cells had high survival rates at −20 °C and 37 °C (Fig. 12 A), and as high as ∼95% at −20 °C, showing that low temperature and 13% KCl did little damage to hepatic stellate cells, which still maintained the biological activity under low temperature.

Meanwhile, hepatic stellate cells were co-incubated with different concentrations of CDs-Ab probe (0, 10, 20, 50, 100, 150 and 200 μg/mL) at −20 °C, MTT results showed that the survival rate of hepatic stellate cells gradually decreased with the increase of probe concentration (Fig. 12B), even under the high concentration of 200 μg/mL probe, the cell survival rate was still more than 80%, showing that the fluorescent probe containing 13% KCl had low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, and could be further used for subsequent cryogenic cell labeling imaging.

In order to explore the targeted labeling effect of CDs-Ab fluorescent probe on hepatic stellate cells at −20 °C, the indirect immunofluorescence imaging method was used, after fixing, permeabilizing and blocking the cells, hepatic stellate cells were incubated with anti-Desmin as primary antibody overnight at −20 °C, then the CDs-Ab as secondary antibody was incubated for 2 h, finally, PI was added to stain the nucleus. Fluorescence microscopy revealed that a large number of hepatic stellate cell nuclei were red (Fig. 12C), and blue fluorescence of the CDs-Ab probe appeared in the cytoplasm. However, the blue fluorescence could hardly be observed in the cells of control group, which were incubated with CDs-Ab probe without a primary antibody, and only red nucleus could be observed, indicating that the CDs-Ab probe could specifically bind to hepatic stellate cells at −20 °C.

In order to explore the selectivity and anti-interference capacity of these CDs, we selected hepatic cells as control cells, and CDs-Ab/anti-Desmin and CDs-Ab were added at −20 °C, respectively. Fluorescence microscopy showed the typical binuclear structure of hepatic cells, indicating that they were both stained by PI with red fluorescence (Fig. 13 ); however, the blue fluorescence of CDs-Ab could hardly be observed in the cytoplasm of hepatic cells whether primary antibody incubation or not. These results proved that the CDs-Ab had good selectivity and anti-interference capacity on hepatic stellate cells.

In order to explore the labeling efficiency of CDs-Ab fluorescent probe on hepatic stellate cells, it was analyzed by flow cytometry. In the experiment, hepatic stellate cells without any probe were used as blank group, the results showed that the fluorescence was low (Fig. 14 A), and 104 was used as the boundary between blank and labeled cells.

Meanwhile, hepatic stellate cells were incubated with anti-Desmin and commercial FITC-Ab as the standard control group, the flow cytometry results showed that the fluorescence intensity of the cells was significantly enhanced (Fig. 14B), and the labeling efficiency was 93.8%. The fluorescence intensity of cells incubated with the anti-Desmin primary antibody and CDs-Ab also increased significantly (Fig. 14C), and the cell labeling efficiency was as high as 89.2%, which was almost the same as that of the standard control group. The cells incubated with only the CDs-Ab were used as negative control, and the results showed that only 6.3% of hepatic stellate cells were labeled (Fig. 14D). The above results indicated that the CDs-Ab probe could specifically target and label hepatic stellate cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging of hepatic stellate cells frozen sections
ce:section.ce:para: In order to label and image the distribution of hepatic stellate cells in liver tissue by the fluorescent probe, frozen sections of fresh liver tissue were obtained. Firstly, anti-Desmin and FITC-Ab labeled liver frozen sections were used as the standard control group for imaging by indirect immunofluorescence method, the results showed that punctate green fluorescence appeared on the liver frozen section (Fig. 15 ), and its fluorescence emission peak was about 522 nm, which was consistent with that of FITC. This was because the hepatic stellate cells were located in the perisinusoidal space, close to the hepatocytes and hepatic sinusoidal endothelial cells, the quantity was rare and accounts for about 10% of the total hepatocytes [40], thus it was sporadically distributed in spots on liver sections.

Next, liver cryosections were labeled with anti-Desmin and CDs-Ab at −20 °C and 37 °C, respectively. The results showed that spots like blue fluorescence appeared on the sections, the arrangement pattern was consistent with that in the standard control group, and its fluorescence spectrum was also consistent with that of the CDs-Ab, indicating that the blue fluorescence was came from CDs-Ab fluorescence probe. The above results further indicated that hepatic stellate cells were successfully labeled with fluorescent probe. At the same time, it was also found that the fluorescence intensity of −20 °C was higher than that of 37 °C. This was because low temperature improved the fluorescence performance of the fluorescent probe, meanwhile, it showed that hepatic stellate cells presented a better labeling effect below the freezing point. At the same time, it could be seen that as a negative control, no obvious punctate blue fluorescence appeared on the liver frozen sections labeled with CDs-Ab without anti-Desmin, and the fluorescence intensity was also very weak, indicating that the CDs-Ab fluorescent probe could specifically target hepatic stellate cells at - 20 °C.

In this paper, multicolor carbon quantum dots were synthesized by hydrothermal method, and CDs-Ab fluorescent probe was prepared after conjugation with antibody. The probe was successfully used to specifically target hepatic stellate cells on liver frozen sections at −20 °C below the freezing point. This study can provide important technical means for understanding liver structure and function.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFA0700402), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 81971658). We also thank the Analytical and Testing Center (HUST), the Research Core Facilities for Life Science (HUST) and the Center for Nanoscale Characterization & Devices (CNCD) at WNLO of HUST for the help of measurement.
